question: Build a search box that calls an API while typing without making unnecessary requests.
answer: Create a FormControl for the search input and listen to its valueChanges. Use debounceTime() to wait until the user pauses typing, distinctUntilChanged() to ignore duplicate searches, and switchMap() to cancel previous API requests and send only the latest one. Display the results using the async pipe.
tip: switchMap() is the most important operator because it automatically cancels outdated API requests.
deep:
What the Interviewer Expects

This question tests your understanding of Reactive Forms, RxJS operators, HTTP requests, and user experience optimization.

How It Works

  1. User types into the search box.
  2. valueChanges emits every keystroke.
  3. debounceTime(300) waits until typing pauses.
  4. distinctUntilChanged() ignores duplicate values.
  5. switchMap() cancels any previous request and starts a new one.
  6. The latest results are displayed using the async pipe.

Why Use switchMap?

Without switchMap(), slow API responses can arrive after newer ones and display outdated results. switchMap() ensures that only the latest search request is processed.

Common Interview Follow-ups

  • Why not call the API on every key press?
  • Why use switchMap() instead of mergeMap()?
  • How would you show a loading spinner while searching?
  • How would you handle API errors?
  • What if the search term is empty?

How to say it in an interview

"I use a Reactive Form control and listen to valueChanges. I apply debounceTime() to reduce API calls, distinctUntilChanged() to avoid duplicate searches, and switchMap() so only the latest request is processed. This improves both performance and user experience."

question: Display a list with client-side pagination (10 items per page).
answer: Store the complete list in memory and maintain the current page number and page size. Calculate the items for the current page using slice(). Generate page numbers dynamically and provide Previous and Next buttons while preventing navigation beyond the first or last page. Never modify the original data; always derive the visible items from it.
tip: Keep the full dataset unchanged. Use slice() to derive the current page instead of modifying the array.
deep:
What the Interviewer Expects

This question tests your understanding of array manipulation, state management, event handling, and rendering lists efficiently.

How It Works

  1. Keep the original list unchanged.
  2. Track the current page number.
  3. Calculate the starting index.
  4. Use slice() to get only the current page.
  5. Render Previous, Next, and page number buttons.

Formula

startIndex = (page - 1) * pageSize
endIndex = startIndex + pageSize

Common Interview Follow-ups

  • How would you disable Previous and Next buttons at the boundaries?
  • How would you implement page size selection?
  • How would you highlight the active page?
  • When would you use server-side pagination instead?
  • How would you preserve the current page after refreshing the browser?

Client-side vs Server-side Pagination

Client-sideServer-side
All data loaded once.Only current page is fetched.
Good for small datasets.Better for large datasets.
Faster page navigation.Lower memory usage.

How to say it in an interview

"I keep the complete dataset unchanged and derive the current page using slice(). The page number and page size determine the visible records. This approach is simple, efficient for small datasets, and avoids mutating the original array."

question: Load more data automatically as the user scrolls to the bottom of the page.
answer: Implement infinite scrolling using an IntersectionObserver. Place a sentinel element at the end of the list and observe when it enters the viewport. When triggered, fetch the next page of data, append it to the existing list, and continue observing until all records have been loaded. Disconnect the observer in ngOnDestroy() to prevent memory leaks.
tip: IntersectionObserver is more efficient than listening to scroll events because the browser handles visibility detection without continuous scroll calculations.
deep:
What the Interviewer Expects

This question tests lazy loading, pagination, browser APIs, performance optimization, and Angular lifecycle hooks.

How It Works

  1. Render the current page of items.
  2. Place a sentinel element at the bottom of the list.
  3. Observe the sentinel using IntersectionObserver.
  4. When it becomes visible, request the next page from the server.
  5. Append the new items to the existing list.
  6. Repeat until there are no more records.

Why Use IntersectionObserver?

Unlike scroll event listeners, IntersectionObserver is optimized by the browser and avoids frequent scroll calculations, making it more efficient for infinite scrolling.

Common Interview Follow-ups

  • How would you prevent duplicate API requests?
  • How would you stop loading when there is no more data?
  • How would you show a loading spinner while fetching the next page?
  • How would you handle API errors?
  • When would you choose Angular CDK Virtual Scroll instead of infinite scrolling?

Infinite Scroll vs Virtual Scroll

Infinite ScrollVirtual Scroll
Continuously appends new data.Renders only visible items.
Good for social feeds and product lists.Best for very large datasets.
DOM grows as more data is loaded.DOM size remains nearly constant.

How to say it in an interview

"I implement infinite scrolling using an IntersectionObserver that watches a sentinel element at the bottom of the list. When it enters the viewport, I fetch the next page of data, append it to the existing list, and stop requesting data when all records have been loaded. This approach is more efficient than listening to scroll events."

question: Create a reusable 5-star rating component.
answer: Create a reusable component that renders five clickable stars. Accept the current rating using @Input() and notify the parent of changes using @Output(). Highlight the selected stars based on the current rating. For Reactive Forms support, implement ControlValueAccessor so the component behaves like a standard Angular form control.
tip: Start with @Input() and @Output(). Implement ControlValueAccessor only when the component needs to work inside Angular forms.
deep:
What the Interviewer Expects

This question tests component communication, event handling, reusable component design, and optionally custom form controls.

Typical Requirements

  • Display five clickable stars.
  • Highlight the selected rating.
  • Allow the parent component to provide the initial value.
  • Notify the parent when the rating changes.
  • Optionally preview the rating on hover.

Implementation Flow

  1. Render the stars using *ngFor.
  2. Compare each star with the current rating.
  3. Update the rating when a star is clicked.
  4. Emit the new value to the parent component.

Common Interview Follow-ups

  • How would you add hover preview?
  • How would you make the component read-only?
  • How would you allow a configurable number of stars?
  • How would you integrate it with Reactive Forms?
  • When should you implement ControlValueAccessor?

Using ControlValueAccessor

If the component needs to work with formControl, formControlName, or [(ngModel)], implement ControlValueAccessor. This allows Angular forms to read and update the component value automatically.

How to say it in an interview

"I would create a reusable component that exposes its value through @Input() and emits changes using @Output(). If the component needs to participate in Angular forms, I'd implement ControlValueAccessor so it behaves like any built-in form control."

question: Create a reusable modal/dialog component.
answer: Create a reusable modal component that can be opened and closed by its parent. Accept the open state using @Input(), notify the parent when it closes using @Output(), and display custom content with ng-content. Close the modal when the user clicks the backdrop or presses the Esc key.
tip: Use ng-content so the modal can display any content instead of being tied to a specific layout.
deep:
What the Interviewer Expects

This question tests reusable component design, parent-child communication, event handling, and content projection.

Typical Requirements

  • Open and close the modal.
  • Close when the backdrop is clicked.
  • Prevent closing when clicking inside the modal.
  • Display dynamic content using ng-content.
  • Close the modal when the Esc key is pressed.

Implementation Flow

  1. Control visibility with an open property.
  2. Project content using ng-content.
  3. Emit a close event so the parent updates its state.
  4. Use stopPropagation() to prevent clicks inside the modal from closing it.

Common Interview Follow-ups

  • How would you animate the modal?
  • How would you prevent background scrolling?
  • How would you trap keyboard focus for accessibility?
  • How would you support multiple modals?
  • Would you use Angular CDK Dialog or build your own?

Accessibility

For production applications, add focus trapping, restore focus when the modal closes, use appropriate ARIA attributes, and support keyboard navigation.

How to say it in an interview

"I would create a reusable modal that receives its open state through @Input(), emits a close event using @Output(), and displays dynamic content with ng-content. I'd also support closing via the backdrop and the Escape key, while considering accessibility features like focus management for production use."

question: Build a todo list (add / toggle / delete / filter).
answer: Create a todo array where each item contains id, text, and done. Implement CRUD operations using immutable array updates: add with a new object, toggle using map(), delete using filter(), and derive filtered views (All, Active, Completed) without modifying the original array. Use trackBy with *ngFor to avoid unnecessary DOM updates. Immutable updates also work well with OnPush change detection.
tip: Interviewers usually expect immutable updates using map(), filter(), and the spread operator instead of mutating the original array.
deep:
What the Interviewer Expects

A Todo application tests your understanding of component state, CRUD operations, event binding, list rendering, and immutable updates.

Typical Features

  • Add a new todo.
  • Mark a todo as completed.
  • Delete a todo.
  • Filter by All, Active, or Completed.

Why Immutable Updates?

Instead of modifying the existing array, create a new one using map(), filter(), and the spread operator. This makes change detection more predictable and works well with OnPush.

OperationMethod
Addspread (...)
Togglemap()
Deletefilter()
Filter ViewDerived getter

Common Interview Follow-ups

  • How would you persist todos using Local Storage or an API?
  • How would you move the state into a service?
  • How would you optimize the list using trackBy?
  • How would you implement editing a todo?
  • How would you implement 'Mark All as Completed'?

How to say it in an interview

"I keep the todo list as component state and perform all updates immutably using map(), filter(), and the spread operator. The filtered list is derived from the original array instead of modifying it, and I use trackBy for efficient rendering. This approach keeps the state predictable and works efficiently with Angular's OnPush change detection."

question: Build a reusable data table with dynamic columns and sorting.
answer: Create a reusable table component that accepts column definitions and row data as inputs. Generate headers and cells dynamically, support sorting when a column header is clicked, and allow custom cell rendering using ng-template and ngTemplateOutlet. Perform sorting on an immutable copy of the data and use trackBy for efficient rendering.
tip: Separate the table structure from cell rendering. ng-template makes the component flexible enough to display any type of data.
deep:
What the Interviewer Expects

This question tests reusable component design, dynamic rendering, sorting, template customization, and Angular performance techniques.

Typical Requirements

  • Generate columns dynamically.
  • Render rows using *ngFor.
  • Sort by clicking column headers.
  • Toggle ascending and descending order.
  • Support custom cell templates.

How It Works

  1. Pass column definitions and row data as inputs.
  2. Generate table headers dynamically.
  3. Sort an immutable copy of the data.
  4. Render custom cells with ngTemplateOutlet.
  5. Use trackBy to minimize DOM updates.

Common Interview Follow-ups

  • How would you add pagination?
  • How would you implement filtering?
  • How would you support row selection?
  • How would you make columns resizable?
  • How would you add server-side sorting?

Production Enhancements

  • Pagination
  • Column filtering
  • Sticky headers
  • Resizable columns
  • Row selection
  • Virtual scrolling
  • Server-side sorting

How to say it in an interview

"I would build a reusable table that receives column definitions and row data as inputs. The component generates the headers dynamically, supports sorting by clicking column headers, allows custom cell rendering using ng-template, and uses immutable updates with trackBy for better performance."

question: Create a reusable tabs component using content projection.
answer: Create a TabComponent that accepts a title and projects its content using ng-content. Build a parent TabsComponent that discovers all child tabs using @ContentChildren, renders the tab headers, tracks the active tab, and displays only the selected tab's content.
tip: @ContentChildren lets a parent component discover projected child components, making it ideal for reusable components like tabs, accordions, and steppers.
deep:
What the Interviewer Expects

This question tests content projection, component communication, Angular queries, and reusable component design.

Typical Requirements

  • Create reusable TabComponent and TabsComponent.
  • Project tab content using ng-content.
  • Display tab headers dynamically.
  • Switch the active tab when a header is clicked.
  • Show only the active tab's content.

How It Works

  1. Each TabComponent exposes a title.
  2. The parent discovers all projected tabs using @ContentChildren.
  3. The parent renders the headers.
  4. Clicking a header changes the active tab.
  5. Only the active tab's projected content is displayed.

Common Interview Follow-ups

  • Why use @ContentChildren instead of @ViewChildren?
  • How would you support dynamically added or removed tabs?
  • How would you lazy-load tab content?
  • How would you animate tab transitions?
  • How would you make the tabs keyboard accessible?

Related Angular Concepts

ConceptPurpose
ng-contentProjects content into a component.
@ContentChildrenAccesses projected child components.
QueryListRepresents the collection of projected tabs.
ngAfterContentInitRuns after projected content becomes available.

How to say it in an interview

"I would create individual TabComponents that project their content using ng-content. The parent TabsComponent collects them with @ContentChildren, renders the tab headers, tracks the active tab, and displays only the selected tab's content."

question: Build a countdown timer that updates every second.
answer: Use RxJS timer() or interval() to emit a value every second. Calculate the remaining time, stop the stream when it reaches zero using takeWhile(), and display the value with the async pipe. This avoids manual subscription management and works well with Angular change detection.
tip: Use the async pipe with RxJS operators like takeWhile() to avoid manual subscription cleanup.
deep:
What the Interviewer Expects

This question tests your understanding of RxJS streams, time-based operators, and Angular's async pipe.

How It Works

  1. Create a timer that emits every second.
  2. Calculate the remaining time.
  3. Stop emitting when the countdown reaches zero.
  4. Display the remaining time using the async pipe.

Why Use RxJS?

Using RxJS eliminates the need for setInterval(), manual cleanup, and explicit subscriptions. The async pipe automatically subscribes and unsubscribes.

Common Interview Follow-ups

  • How would you add Start, Pause, and Reset functionality?
  • How would you restart the timer?
  • How would you display the timer in mm:ss format?
  • How would you implement a Resend OTP timer?
  • When would you use interval() instead of timer()?

timer() vs interval()

OperatorBehavior
timer(0, 1000)Emits immediately, then every second.
interval(1000)Waits one second before the first emission.

How to say it in an interview

"I would use RxJS timer() to emit values every second, calculate the remaining time using map(), stop the stream with takeWhile(), and bind it to the template using the async pipe. This avoids manual subscription management and integrates cleanly with Angular."

question: Build a dynamic form from a JSON configuration.
answer: Create a form dynamically from a configuration object. Generate FormControls and FormGroups based on the field definitions, apply validators from the configuration, and render the appropriate input component using *ngFor and ngSwitch. This approach allows forms to be generated without changing the component code.
tip: The key idea is that the UI is driven by metadata rather than hardcoded form controls.
deep:
What the Interviewer Expects

This question tests Reactive Forms, dynamic component rendering, metadata-driven UI, and scalable form design.

Typical Requirements

  • Read form fields from a JSON configuration.
  • Create controls dynamically.
  • Apply validators from the configuration.
  • Render different input types.
  • Submit the generated form.

Common Interview Follow-ups

  • How would you support dropdowns and checkboxes?
  • How would you implement conditional fields?
  • How would you load the schema from an API?
  • How would you add custom validators?
  • How would you render custom components?

How to say it in an interview

"Instead of hardcoding controls, I generate the form from a configuration object. The metadata defines the fields, validators, and input types, making the form reusable and easy to extend."

question: Display different content based on a radio button selection.
answer: Bind all radio buttons to a single property using [(ngModel)]. When the selected value changes, Angular automatically updates the model. Use @if, *ngIf, or @switch to conditionally render the appropriate content based on the selected option.
tip: Use a single model property as the source of truth. The selected radio button and the displayed content should always be driven by the same value.
code: selected = 'A';
deep:
What the Interviewer Expects

This question tests two-way data binding, template-driven forms, and conditional rendering in Angular.

Typical Requirements

  • Display multiple radio buttons.
  • Bind them using [(ngModel)].
  • Show different content based on the selected option.
  • Keep the UI synchronized with the component state.

Example

<input
  type="radio"
  name="option"
  value="A"
  [(ngModel)]="selected"
> Option A

<input
  type="radio"
  name="option"
  value="B"
  [(ngModel)]="selected"
> Option B

@if (selected === 'A') {
  <div>Content for Option A</div>
} @else {
  <div>Content for Option B</div>
}

Real-world Examples

  • Payment method selection (UPI, Card, Net Banking).
  • User type (Customer or Admin).
  • Delivery option (Pickup or Home Delivery).
  • Personal vs Business account.

Common Interview Follow-ups

  • How would you implement this using Reactive Forms?
  • When would you use @switch instead of multiple @if blocks?
  • Can each section be a separate Angular component?
  • How would you preserve the state of hidden sections?

How to say it in an interview

"I bind all radio buttons to a single model property using [(ngModel)]. Based on the selected value, I conditionally render the appropriate section using @if, *ngIf, or @switch. This ensures the UI always stays synchronized with the component state."

question: Fetch employees from an API and display only their id and name.
answer: Call the employee API and use the RxJS map() operator to transform the response before it reaches the component. Inside the RxJS map(), use JavaScript's Array.map() to create a new array containing only the required fields.
tip: Remember that RxJS map() transforms the Observable, while Array.map() transforms the items inside the array.
deep:
What the Interviewer Expects

This question tests your understanding of HTTP requests, RxJS operators, and data transformation before rendering it in the UI.

How It Works

  1. Fetch employee data from an API.
  2. Use RxJS map() to transform the Observable.
  3. Use JavaScript Array.map() to reshape each employee object.
  4. Display the transformed data in the template.

Common Interview Follow-ups

  • Why transform data in the service instead of the component?
  • What's the difference between RxJS map() and JavaScript Array.map()?
  • When would you use switchMap() instead of map()?

How to say it in an interview

"I use the RxJS map() operator to transform the API response before it reaches the component. Inside it, I use JavaScript's Array.map() to reshape each object into the format required by the UI."

question: Fetch user details, then load the user's orders.
answer: When one API depends on the result of another, use switchMap(). The first request retrieves the user details, and the returned user ID is passed to the second request to fetch the user's orders. switchMap() automatically cancels any previous in-progress request if a new one starts.
tip: Use switchMap() for dependent API calls. Use concatMap() when every request must complete in order without cancellation.
deep:
What the Interviewer Expects

This question tests HTTP requests, RxJS flattening operators, asynchronous programming, and dependent API execution.

How It Works

  1. Fetch the user details.
  2. Extract the user ID from the response.
  3. Pass the ID to the second API.
  4. Display the user's orders after both requests complete.

Why Use switchMap?

switchMap() automatically cancels any previous inner Observable when a new outer emission occurs. This prevents outdated requests from updating the UI and is ideal for user-driven actions such as searches or route changes.

Common Interview Follow-ups

  • Why use switchMap() instead of nested subscriptions?
  • When would you use concatMap()?
  • When would mergeMap() be a better choice?
  • How would you handle errors from either API?
  • How would you load three dependent APIs?

switchMap vs concatMap

switchMap()concatMap()
Cancels previous requests.Queues requests and preserves order.
Best for searches and route changes.Best when every request must complete.

How to say it in an interview

"When one API depends on the result of another, I use switchMap() to flatten the Observable chain. It avoids nested subscriptions, keeps the code readable, and automatically cancels stale requests when a new operation begins."

question: Load data from multiple APIs in parallel and display the page after all responses are received.
answer: Use forkJoin() to execute multiple independent API calls at the same time. It waits until every Observable completes, then emits a single object or array containing all responses. This is ideal for loading dashboard or page data that depends on multiple APIs.
tip: forkJoin() emits only once after every Observable completes, making it similar to Promise.all().
deep:
What the Interviewer Expects

This question tests your understanding of parallel API execution, RxJS combinators, and coordinating multiple asynchronous operations.

How It Works

  1. Start multiple independent HTTP requests.
  2. Execute them in parallel.
  3. Wait until every request completes.
  4. Receive all responses together.
  5. Update the UI once with the combined data.

When to Use

  • Dashboard pages.
  • User profile with related data.
  • Loading multiple dropdown values.
  • Initial page data from several APIs.

Common Interview Follow-ups

  • What happens if one API request fails?
  • What's the difference between forkJoin() and combineLatest()?
  • When would you use zip() instead?
  • How would you handle errors without failing the entire request?
  • Why is forkJoin() similar to Promise.all()?

forkJoin vs combineLatest

forkJoin()combineLatest()
Emits once after all Observables complete.Emits whenever any source emits after all have emitted once.
Ideal for HTTP requests.Ideal for long-lived streams such as form controls or WebSockets.

How to say it in an interview

"I use forkJoin() when multiple API calls are independent and the page should render only after all responses are available. It executes the requests in parallel and emits a single result once every Observable completes."

question: Load data once and share it across multiple components without making duplicate API calls.
answer: Create a shared service that fetches the data only once and exposes it as an Observable. Cache the result using shareReplay(1) or store it in a BehaviorSubject. Multiple components subscribe to the same stream, ensuring that the HTTP request is executed only once.
tip: shareReplay(1) is ideal for caching HTTP responses. Use BehaviorSubject when components also need to update the shared state.
deep:
What the Interviewer Expects

This question tests service design, HTTP caching, state sharing, RxJS multicasting, and Angular dependency injection.

Typical Requirements

  • Call the API only once.
  • Share the same data across multiple components.
  • Avoid duplicate HTTP requests.
  • Keep the data synchronized.

Approaches

ApproachBest For
shareReplay(1)Caching HTTP responses.
BehaviorSubjectShared mutable application state.

Common Interview Follow-ups

  • When would you choose BehaviorSubject over shareReplay(1)?
  • How would you refresh the cached data?
  • How would you invalidate the cache after an update?
  • What happens if the API request fails?
  • How would you implement this using Angular Signals?

How to say it in an interview

"I keep the shared data inside a singleton service. For HTTP caching I prefer shareReplay(1) because it shares one request across all subscribers. If the data needs to be updated by multiple components, I use a BehaviorSubject to manage the shared state."

question: Build an autocomplete search component without using Angular Material.
answer: Create an input backed by a FormControl and listen to valueChanges. Use debounceTime(), distinctUntilChanged(), and switchMap() to fetch matching suggestions from an API. Display the suggestions in a dropdown, allow the user to select an item, and hide the dropdown when an item is selected or the input loses focus.
tip: The important parts are debouncing input, cancelling stale requests with switchMap(), and correctly managing the dropdown state.
deep:
What the Interviewer Expects

This question tests Reactive Forms, RxJS, HTTP requests, reusable component design, and user interaction.

Typical Requirements

  • Search while typing.
  • Debounce user input.
  • Cancel previous API requests.
  • Display matching suggestions.
  • Select an item from the dropdown.
  • Hide the dropdown when appropriate.

How It Works

  1. Listen to valueChanges.
  2. Wait briefly using debounceTime().
  3. Ignore duplicate values using distinctUntilChanged().
  4. Fetch suggestions using switchMap().
  5. Render the dropdown.
  6. Update the input when an option is selected.

Common Interview Follow-ups

  • How would you support keyboard navigation (↑ ↓ Enter)?
  • How would you highlight the matching text?
  • How would you display a loading indicator?
  • How would you cache previous search results?
  • How would you convert this into a reusable form control using ControlValueAccessor?

Production Enhancements

  • Minimum character limit before searching.
  • Loading spinner.
  • No results message.
  • Keyboard accessibility.
  • Click outside to close.
  • Result caching.
  • Highlighted matching text.

How to say it in an interview

"I use a Reactive Form control with valueChanges, apply debounceTime(), distinctUntilChanged(), and switchMap() to fetch suggestions efficiently. The component manages the dropdown state, supports item selection, and avoids unnecessary API calls."

question: Reorder a list using Angular CDK Drag & Drop.
answer: Use Angular CDK's cdkDropList and cdkDrag directives to make list items draggable. Handle the cdkDropListDropped event and reorder the array using moveItemInArray(). For dragging items between different lists, use transferArrayItem().
tip: Use moveItemInArray() for reordering within the same list and transferArrayItem() when moving items between lists.
deep:
What the Interviewer Expects

This question tests your understanding of Angular CDK, event handling, list rendering, and state management.

Typical Requirements

  • Display a draggable list.
  • Reorder items within the same list.
  • Optionally move items between multiple lists.
  • Update the underlying array after dropping an item.

How It Works

  1. Apply cdkDropList to the container.
  2. Apply cdkDrag to each item.
  3. Listen for cdkDropListDropped.
  4. Update the array using moveItemInArray() or transferArrayItem().

Common Interview Follow-ups

  • How would you drag items between two lists?
  • How would you save the new order to the backend?
  • How would you disable dragging for specific items?
  • How would you implement a Trello-style board?
  • What advantages does Angular CDK provide over the native HTML5 Drag & Drop API?

Important APIs

APIPurpose
cdkDropListDefines a drop zone.
cdkDragMakes an element draggable.
moveItemInArray()Reorders items within the same list.
transferArrayItem()Moves items between different lists.

How to say it in an interview

"I use Angular CDK Drag & Drop by applying cdkDropList to the container and cdkDrag to each item. On drop, I update the underlying array using moveItemInArray() for reordering or transferArrayItem() when moving items between lists."

question: Build a recursive Tree View component to display nested comments or folders.
answer: Create a reusable recursive component that accepts a node as an input. Display the current node and recursively render its child nodes using the same component. Use *ngFor to iterate through the children and stop recursion when a node has no children.
tip: The key idea is that a component can render itself recursively until there are no more child nodes.
deep:
What the Interviewer Expects

This question tests recursive components, hierarchical data rendering, component communication, and Angular template recursion.

Typical Requirements

  • Display hierarchical data.
  • Render child nodes recursively.
  • Stop recursion when there are no children.
  • Keep the component reusable.

Real-world Examples

  • Nested comments (Reddit, YouTube).
  • Folder/File explorer.
  • Organization charts.
  • Category trees.
  • Navigation menus.

Common Interview Follow-ups

  • How would you expand/collapse nodes?
  • How would you lazy-load child nodes?
  • How would you highlight the selected node?
  • How would you add drag-and-drop support?
  • How would you optimize rendering for very large trees?

Production Enhancements

  • Expand/Collapse support.
  • Lazy loading of child nodes.
  • Search and highlight matching nodes.
  • Checkbox selection.
  • Virtual scrolling for large trees.
  • TrackBy for better performance.

How to say it in an interview

"I create a reusable component that accepts a tree node as input. The component displays the current node and recursively renders each child using the same component until there are no more child nodes. This approach naturally fits hierarchical data like comments, folders, and menus."

question: Build a Kanban board where users can drag tasks between columns.
answer: Create multiple cdkDropList containers (Todo, In Progress, Done) and render tasks inside each column using cdkDrag. Allow users to reorder tasks within a column using moveItemInArray() and move tasks between columns using transferArrayItem(). Update the underlying state whenever a task is moved.
tip: Interviewers expect you to use Angular CDK Drag & Drop and correctly update the data model after each drag operation.
deep:
What the Interviewer Expects

This question tests Angular CDK Drag & Drop, component interaction, state management, and handling complex UI interactions.

Typical Requirements

  • Create multiple draggable columns.
  • Allow reordering tasks within a column.
  • Allow moving tasks between columns.
  • Update the application state after each drag operation.

Real-world Examples

  • Trello
  • Jira
  • Azure DevOps Boards
  • GitHub Projects

Common Interview Follow-ups

  • How would you persist changes to the backend?
  • How would you support drag handles?
  • How would you prevent dropping into certain columns?
  • How would you implement optimistic updates?
  • How would you handle thousands of cards efficiently?

Production Enhancements

  • Persist board changes via an API.
  • Drag handles.
  • Column WIP limits.
  • Auto-scroll while dragging.
  • Keyboard accessibility.
  • Virtual scrolling for large boards.
  • Undo/Redo support.

How to say it in an interview

"I would use Angular CDK Drag & Drop to create draggable columns. Tasks can be reordered within a column using moveItemInArray() and moved across columns using transferArrayItem(). After each drag event, I update the application state and optionally persist the changes to the backend."

question: Implement a Light / Dark theme switcher with persistent user preference.
answer: Create a ThemeService to manage the application's current theme. Toggle a CSS class on the document body or root element, save the selected theme in localStorage, and restore the saved preference when the application starts.
tip: Keep theme logic inside a service so every component can react to theme changes without duplicating code.
deep:
What the Interviewer Expects

This question tests state management, service design, DOM manipulation, persistence, and application initialization.

Typical Requirements

  • Switch between Light and Dark themes.
  • Apply the selected theme to the entire application.
  • Persist the user's preference.
  • Restore the theme after refreshing the page.

How It Works

  1. User clicks the theme toggle.
  2. The service updates the current theme.
  3. A CSS class is added or removed from the application's root element.
  4. The selected theme is stored in localStorage.
  5. The saved theme is restored during application startup.

Common Interview Follow-ups

  • Why use a service instead of putting the logic in a component?
  • How would you detect the user's preferred system theme?
  • How would you synchronize the theme across browser tabs?
  • How would you support multiple themes instead of just light and dark?
  • Would you use CSS variables or separate stylesheets?

Production Considerations

  • Respect the system preference using prefers-color-scheme.
  • Avoid the initial flash of the wrong theme by applying the saved theme before the app renders.
  • Prefer CSS variables for scalable theming.

How to say it in an interview

"I keep the theme state inside a shared service. When the user changes the theme, I update a CSS class on the application's root element, persist the preference in localStorage, and restore it during application initialization so the user's choice is preserved across sessions."

question: Build a Login Page with username and password validation.
answer: Create a Reactive Form with username and password controls. Apply validators such as required, email, and minLength. Display validation errors only after the user interacts with a field or submits the form. On successful validation, process the login request.
tip: Most Angular interviews expect Reactive Forms because they are easier to validate, test, and maintain.
deep:
What the Interviewer Expects

This question tests your understanding of Reactive Forms, validators, error handling, event binding, and form submission.

Typical Requirements

  • Username (or email) is required.
  • Password is required.
  • Password must have a minimum length.
  • Show validation messages only after the field is touched or the form is submitted.
  • Disable the Login button until the form is valid (optional).

Validation Example

<input formControlName="username">
@if ((submitted || loginForm.controls.username.touched) && loginForm.controls.username.hasError('required')) {
  <small>Username is required</small>
}

<input type="password" formControlName="password">
@if ((submitted || loginForm.controls.password.touched) && loginForm.controls.password.hasError('minlength')) {
  <small>Minimum 6 characters</small>
}

Common Interview Follow-ups

  • How would you call a login API after validation?
  • How would you display backend validation errors?
  • How would you disable the Login button while the request is in progress?
  • How would you implement Remember Me?

How to say it in an interview

"I use Reactive Forms with built-in validators like required, email, and minLength. Validation messages are shown only after user interaction or form submission. Once the form is valid, I send the credentials to the backend for authentication."
